coredata.dc:description: Due to the widespread of pathogens and infectious diseases, rapid detection and antibacterial agents are needed. Antibacterial nanomaterials have attracted attention due to their advantageous properties and unique mechanisms. Besides, the green route approach to synthesizing nanoparticles has attracted interest due to being environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and non-toxic. In this study, carbon dots (Cdots), a promising antibacterial material, have been combined with the Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the carrier and produce Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposite via green synthesis for bacteria detection and antibacterial agents. Green synthesis was achieved with Moringa oleifera leaves extract as reducing agent on Fe3O4 and watermelon peel as carbon source on Cdots. X-ray diffraction shows a cubic inverse spinel structure of magnetite. The phase composition of the nanocomposites consists of magnetite and carbon. The particle size of nanocomposites is 13.4 nm with semi-spherical morphology and high surface area. The bonding analysis and element composition are consistent with phase composition estimation that confirms the existence of Fe3O4 and Cdots on the nanocomposites and lacks other impurities. The vibrating sample magnetometer shows the superparamagnetic behavior of the samples. Nanocomposites were used to detect Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria. The quenching of photoluminescence of the nanocomposite shows an increase in the amount of E. coli bacteria. Furthermore, nanocomposites produce high inhibition zones against E. coli bacteria within 48 h, offering excellent antibacterial properties. Hence, nanocomposites provide a promising agent for bacteria detection and antibacterial treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the widespread of pathogens and infectious diseases, rapid detection and antibacterial agents are needed. Antibacterial nanomaterials have attracted attention due to their advantageous properties and unique mechanisms. Besides, the green route approach to synthesizing nanoparticles has attracted interest due to being environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and non-toxic. In this study, carbon dots (Cdots), a promising antibacterial material, have been combined with the Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the carrier and produce Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposite via green synthesis for bacteria detection and antibacterial agents. Green synthesis was achieved with Moringa oleifera leaves extract as reducing agent on Fe3O4 and watermelon peel as carbon source on Cdots. X-ray diffraction shows a cubic inverse spinel structure of magnetite. The phase composition of the nanocomposites consists of magnetite and carbon. The particle size of nanocomposites is 13.4 nm with semi-spherical morphology and high surface area. The bonding analysis and element composition are consistent with phase composition estimation that confirms the existence of Fe3O4 and Cdots on the nanocomposites and lacks other impurities. The vibrating sample magnetometer shows the superparamagnetic behavior of the samples. Nanocomposites were used to detect Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria. The quenching of photoluminescence of the nanocomposite shows an increase in the amount of E. coli bacteria. Furthermore, nanocomposites produce high inhibition zones against E. coli bacteria within 48 h, offering excellent antibacterial properties. Hence, nanocomposites provide a promising agent for bacteria detection and antibacterial treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing human populations and industrial growth cause pollution and waste. The waste can contaminate water, resulting in widespread pathogens and infectious diseases [1]. Antibiotics are commonly used to maneuver bacteria pathogens [2]. Unfortunately, applying antibiotics to bacteria faces another global health issue due to the drug-resistant bacteria. Bacteria can change the nature of the cell wall, becoming unrecognizable to the antibiotic, which makes the antibiotics less effective [1,3]. Besides, bacteria detection, such as bacterial metabolic tests and ultra-sensitive detection based on nucleic acid amplification, is time-consuming and uses expensive equipment, which makes it inefficient [4,5]. Therefore, a significant concern is developing rapid and easy detection of bacteria pathogens and alternative antibacterial agents to fight drug-resistant bacteria.

Nowadays, antibacterial agent-based nanomaterial has great attention due to its physicochemical properties and unique dimensions [6]. The large surface area of nanomaterial has the potential to interact with microorganisms, which makes it more effective than conventional antibiotics [6,7]. Carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots (Cdots) are “zero-dimensional” sizes, less toxic, biocompatible, and are stable in an aqueous environment that is promising as antibacterial agents [8–10]. The other unique characteristic of Cdots is excellent photostability or fluorescence that supports bacteria detection [11–15]. Cdots can be prepared by simple and low-cost methods such as hydrothermal and microwave [16,17]. Tariq et al. show that Cdots from figs fruit synthesis via microwave can rupture the Escherichia coli bacteria (E.coli) [3]. In other work by Gedda et al., Cdots were successfully synthesized from Azadirachta indica leaves extract and promising as bioimaging, antifungal and antibacterial against gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Bacillus subtilis) and gram-negative bacteria (E.coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) [18]. Saravanan et al. also reported antibacterial activity against E.coli and S. aureus after 24 h incubation using Cdots from turmeric leaves via the one-step hydrothermal method [9]. However, Cdots in the pristine form are hard to eliminate from the solution due to their small size and deficiency polarity [19]. In addition, Cdots cannot be controlled for their mobility and low activity on the release system, limiting its application [20]. The essential key issues for future application of Cdots as an antibacterial agent and bacteria detection are promoting easy mobility and carrier system, separating from the after-killed bacteria, and evolving the reusability.

Composite materials were introduced to overcome the above problems. Cdots need to combine with other materials to improve their antibacterial properties, especially in controlled mobility and separation. Iron oxide nanoparticles such as magnetite (Fe3O4) have become prominent due to their superparamagnetic performance, biocompatibility, and large surface area [21–23]. The superparamagnetic feature can promote Fe3O4 on the easy magnetic separation properties and high reusability [7,24]. Besides, the other features of Fe3O4 are not harmful to human cells and also encourage various biomedical applications such as antibacterial, targeted anticancer, drug loading, and release [25–28]. In the antibacterial application, Fe3O4 support the formation of reactive oxygen species, although in low amounts, and can release metal ion to bind with protein groups and enzymes to destroy bacterial cell wall, which can induce bacteria death [29,30]. Incorporated Cdots with Fe3O4 will produce superior features such as high specific surface area, great magnetic properties, excellent photoluminescent behavior, and non-toxic, which propose multifunction material.

Nanocomposite Fe3O4/Cdots were successfully applied in many fields, such as magnetically separable photocatalysts, to degrade pollutants in water that can achieve high degradation in a short time [31,32]. Nanocomposites were used as electrochemical sensors for mood disorders [33], rapid colorimetric detection of glutathione [34], sensitive detection of glucose [35], recyclable fluorescence detection and purification of methcathinone in sewage [36], and electron transfer [37]. Besides, the features of nanocomposites were also ideal as bacteria detection and antibacterial agents for gram-positive as well as gram-negative bacteria. Stachowska et al. synthesize carbon dots/iron oxide via thermal decomposition under reflux at 230 °C for 3 h, showing low cytotoxicity and fluorescent properties. The nanocomposite can be used as a probe for diagnostic tools, which can be directed via an external magnetic field to targeted destinations. Carbon dots/iron oxide also show antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus [38]. In another study, Kuang et al. successfully constructed biocompatible Fe3O4/Cdots via hydrothermal treatment for 24 h at 210 °C as a carrier of curcumin with inherent fluorescence, potentially a therapeutic candidate for Alzheimer's treatment [20]. Previous studies used high temperature and prolonged synthesis reactions that consume high energy. These conventional methods also use expensive chemical reagents and can produce harmful by-products [39]. Therefore, a study challenge remains to develop a facile green-synthesis approach on prepared Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites to minimize the above drawbacks.

Green synthesis was a cost-effective method utilizing the plant part to produce non-toxic and environmentally friendly material [40,41]. Leaves extract is a popular precursor of green synthesis nanoparticles due to its phytochemical contents. Moringa oleifera (MO) [42,43] and Murrayakonenigii leaves [44] have phytochemicals that act as reducing agents and stabilizers in producing nanoparticles, especially magnetic nanoparticles [45,46]. Besides, green synthesis on Cdots was achieved by utilizing plants part [15,47] and biomass [48,49] as precursors. Fini et al. have successfully produced Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposite via hydrothermal using lemon extract for fluorescence signals of different concentrations of E. coli [4]. However, the study of green synthesis of Fe3O4/Cdots for bacteria detection and antibacterial agents is still lacking.

In this report, Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites were synthesized via green route utilizing MO and watermelon peel waste for simple and rapid bacteria detection and antibacterial agent. The effect of Cdots concentration on the microstructures, optical, and magnetic properties of the Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites was also investigated comprehensively. The green-synthesized Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites were used for E.coli bacteria detection. Moreover, the zone of inhibition test or Kirby–Bauer test was also investigated to study the antibacterial properties.

FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, NH4OH 10 %, ethanol 96 %, and distilled water were purchased from Merck (Germany) and Sigma Aldrich (USA). These chemicals precursor are analytical grade without further purification. MO leaf extract were produced by PT. Timurasa, Indonesia. Watermelon peel were purchased from the local market (Indonesia).

In brief, 5 g MO powder dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water and heated at 60 °C (see Fig. 1 (a)). After 60 min of stirring, the MO solution was cooled to room temperature. Then, the MO solution was filtered using filter paper and kept in the refrigerator for the next step.

Fig. 1(b) shows the synthesis of Fe3O4 using the co-precipitation method. First, 4.054 g and 2.086 g of FeCl3·6H2O and FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved in distilled water. After 15 min of stirring, 10 mL of MO solution was added, keeping the temperature steady at 60 °C under 30 min of stirring. The addition of MO makes the greenish solution. Next, 30 mL of NH4OH solution with 10 % concentration was dropwise on the mixture for 90 min. The formation of Fe3O4 was shown by the changes in the mixture color from green to black. A black precipitate was observed in the bottom of the glass after cooling to room temperature. The precipitate was washed with distilled water and separated using an external magnet seven times until the pH reached 7. Finally, the precipitates were dried at 100 °C for 2 h and produced Fe3O4 powder.

Cdots were synthesized by facile hydrothermal method utilizing biomass, namely watermelon peel. First, mix 15 mL of watermelon peel juice with 15 mL of ethanol and 15 mL of distilled water and stir for 30 min. Then, the pulp-like mixture was transferred to an autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 3 h. Next, after the temperature reached 34 °C, the Cdots solution was filtered using filter paper and produced Cdots solution. This process can be seen in Fig. 1(c).

Nanocomposites were fabricated by a simple sonication method. In brief, 0.8 g of Fe3O4 was dissolved in 10 mL and 20 mL of Cdots solution to obtain Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 %, respectively. The mixtures were sonicated for 30 min and then isolated overnight. The next day, the mixture was washed with distilled water and separated using an external magnet three times. Finally, the precipitate was dried at 90 °C for 4 h and obtained Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites (see Fig. 1(d)).

The Fe3O4, Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % were characterized using X-Ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XD-3H) to obtain the crystal structure, lattice parameter, and phase composition. The morphology, particle size, and diffraction ring of the samples were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-1400). Scanning electron microscope – energy dispersive X-ray (SEM-EDX, JSM-6510LA) were used to analyze the surface morphology and chemical element of the samples. The specific surface area were calculated using Brunauer, Emmett, Teller (BET) analysis from a N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm (BET, Quantachrome Instruments St 1 on NOVA touch 4LX). Determination of functional groups or bonding analysis were conducted by Fourier transform infra-red spectrophotometer (FTIR, Prestige Shimadzu-21). The magnetic properties of the samples were characterized by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Riken Denshi Co. Ltd.). Finally, the absorbance capability and band gap energy were examined by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, Shimadzu Spectrophotometer UV-1900).

Bacteria detection was conducted using photoluminescence and E. coli bacteria. First, Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites were dispersed in distilled water and sonication for 30 min. At the same time, E. coli dispersed in distilled water was diluted twice to obtain the ratios of 1/10 and 1/100 by log dilution scale. The dilution ratio corresponds to reducing bacteria concentration. Next, bacteria suspensions were mixed with Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites, and quantitative detection was conducted by photoluminescence intensity.

The antibacterial activity test of Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites was performed using agar diffusion and gram-negative bacteria (E. coli). Mueller-Hinton Agar (MHA) plates were chosen as the medium for this study. E. coli in suspension form was inoculated on the MHA and waited a few minutes until it dried. Fe3O4/Cdots were put on the surface medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 and 48 h. After incubation, the zone of inhibition that formed around the samples on the plate was measured.

Fig. 2 shows the Fe3O4 nanoparticle formation mechanism via the green approach, classified into three steps. The first step is activation, which consists of bioreduction and nucleation. In this step, FeCl3·6H2O and FeSO4·7H2O precursors supply Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions. MO extract contains biologically active compounds such as flavonoids and phenolics. Flavonoids have abundant hydroxyl bonds that have lower association energy than other bonds. It leads to release hydrogen atoms and interact with Fe ions [45]. The interaction promotes Fe ions reduction from Fe3+ and Fe2+ to Fe0. Reduced Fe were formed nuclei that called nucleation processes. The second step of green synthesis is the growth step, in which nuclei are associated with forming nanoparticles to specific morphology. The size of nanoparticles increases, accompanied by an increasing aggregation barrier. When the barrier energy is higher than the thermal energy of the nanoparticles, the possibility of particle aggregation is reduced. In other words, the probability of the coalescence process between large particles is lower than two small particles [50]. Therefore, the nanoparticles get to the third step, called termination. In this step, MO extract also plays an important role as a stabilizing agent to achieve maximum activity. Finally, nanoparticles have terminated with a stable size and a certain morphology.

The study of the mechanism of Cdots formation is still being developed. The synthesis of Cdots using the green approach is included in the bottom-up method. The formation of Cdots from organic molecules is generally divided into three stages: condensation, polymerization, and carbonization [51]. In this study, ethanol and distilled water can dissolve organic molecules of watermelon peel. Small organic molecules from watermelon peels, such as polyphenols were associated and formed new compounds with longer bonds or chains through condensation reactions. The compounds will further polymerization or combination to form carbon groups through covalent, non-covalent or other interactions. At certain temperatures, the carbonization occurs, modifying the polymer-like carbon chains to carbon cores. Some hydroxyl groups and others (passivation agents) become part of the surface state that plays an important role in the luminescence ability of Cdots. Fig. 3 shows the formation of Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposite. Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites are formed by combining Fe3O4 nanoparticle powder and Cdots solution, which is synthesized via a green synthesis. Sonication assists in dispersing Fe3O4 in the Cdots solution, which can increase interactions between nanoparticles. Due to the favorable interactions, Cdots were anchoring on the Fe3O4 surface spontaneously or self-assembly to form a Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposite [33].

The crystal structure of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites samples were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and analyzed by the Rietveld refinement method to produce the XRD pattern as shown in Fig. 4 . The diffraction pattern of the samples confirm a cubic inverse spinel structure of magnetite according to the standard XRD data (ICDD No.01–075-0033) with the main peak at 2θ = 35.5° was assigned to the (311) plane. For the nanocomposite sample, Cdots were detected as a small peak at ∼ 23.9o , which indexed to (002) plane [32,52,53]. The increasing Cdots concentration on nanocomposites affects the decreasing crystallinity of the Fe3O4, which is signified by broadening and reducing the magnetite intensity peak [54]. The decreasing crystallinity of magnetite also indicates that Cdots, as amorphous material, were embedded on the surface of Fe3O4. Table 1 . shows the estimate of the phase composition of the samples that confirm the formation of Fe3O4/Cdots. The percentage value was appropriate with the diffraction peak of the samples. Furthermore, the lattice parameter values for Fe3O4, Fe3O4/Cdots 50 %, and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % are 8.16, 8.15, and 8.20 Å, respectively. Fabrication of nanocomposite allows incorporating interaction or collision between Fe3O4 and Cdots, which might lead to crystal distortion on the interface and change the lattice parameter of Fe3O4, which impacts the change of the strain. The strain causes a broadening peak, affecting the decreasing crystallite size [55]. The Debye-Scherrer equation was used to determine the crystallite size of Fe3O4, Fe3O4/Cdots 50 %, and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % are 10.8, 10.1, and 8.9 nm, respectively.

TEM observed the morphology and size distribution of Cdots. Fig. 5 shows that Cdots have a spherical shape and well dispersion (dark spot). In addition, the average size diameter of Cdots was 6.6 nm. The small size and spherical shape of Cdots can increase the surface area feature of nanoparticles. Cdots are good solubility and can easily react with other materials to form nanocomposites [16]. These characteristics also support the potential for effective bacteria detection and antibacterial agents.

Fig. 6 shows the TEM images with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) and size distribution estimation of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/Cdots 50 %. In the presented TEM images for Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % exhibit semi-spherical shapes. The diffraction rings on the SAED images show polycrystalline and are consistent with XRD results. However, the unclear view of the nanocomposite is due to the Cdots nanoparticle coated on the surface of Fe3O4. The small size of Cdots on the surface of Fe3O4 affects the high reactive surface area and van der Waals forces interaction between particles [44]. In addition, the size of Fe3O4 is 14.6 nm, while the measurement of nanocomposite resulted in an average diameter of 13.4 nm. Furthermore, the size distribution of nanocomposites also shows relatively uniform size. The decreasing and homogeneous particle size of nanocomposites related to the large surface area is favorable to futuristic applications such as bacteria detection and antibacterial agents.

Fig. 7 shows the EDX spectrum, SEM images, and mapping analysis of Fe3O4, Fe3O4/Cdots 50 %, and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 %. EDX spectrum detected the percentage of Fe and O that confirm the existence of Fe3O4 (Fig. 7(a)). The surface morphology of Fe3O4with 3000X magnification is relatively smooth due to the single phase, but also clump and agglomerate. The mapping analysis in Fig. 7(b) reveals that the Fe and O elements were well distributed. Furthermore, the EDX spectrum of nanocomposites identifies Fe, O, and C (Fig. 7(c) and (e)). The Fe and O are the elements of Fe3O4, while C is the element of Cdots. The weight percentage of C in the selected area of Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % are 10.9 % and 12 %, respectively. It can be seen that the nanocomposite with a high concentration of Cdots has the highest C elements, confirming that nanocomposites were successfully fabricated. This data also corresponds to phase composition estimation by XRD analysis. In general, SEM images of nanocomposite (Fig. 7(d) and (f)) show relative agglomerate due to the smaller size of the particle as mentioned in TEM analysis. The surface morphology of nanocomposites is also brighter and rougher than Fe3O4 due to Cdots attachment and higher water content [56,57]. Mapping analysis of nanocomposites shows both samples were dominated by Fe followed by O, while C elements were distributed on the surface of Fe3O4 and had no impurities on the structure.

The Brunauer, Emmett, Teller (BET) analysis were perfomed to estimate the surface area of nanocomposites, which shown in Fig. 8 . The calculation data obtain the BET surface areas for Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % were 92.9 and 100.9 m2/g, respectively. This results are higher than the previous study that report the Fe3O4/Cdots has 62 m2/g [58]. The surface area of the nanocomposites increases with increasing Cdots concentration on the surface of Fe3O4. These results can support the TEM result and other characteristic that the smaller particle size of the nanocomposites follows higher surface area. The relatively high specific surface area proved that the prepared nanocomposites have potential and are suitable for bacteria detection and antibacterial agents.

The FTIR spectra on Fig. 9 reveal the samples' functional group, which is essential for understanding nanocomposite formation and involves the antibacterial mechanism. The profile spectrum of nanocomposite was appropriate with Fe3O4 and Cdots spectrum. the deep vibration in the range of 630 − 579 cm−1 related to Fe − O stretching from Fe3O4 [22,59]. Fe3O4 /Cdots 55 % and Fe3O4 /Cdots 71 % detected a deep vibration at 1635 and 1632 cm−1, respectively, assigned to C = C stretching. The abundance of O-H bond is also detected on the nanocomposites at 3425 cm−1, giving high nanocomposites solubility [60]. The C = C bond corresponds to the core of Cdots, while the O-H bond represents the surface state [32]. The indication of Cdots formation is also confirmed by the presence of C-O bond at 1394 and 1399 cm−1 for Fe3O4 /Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4 /Cdots 70 %, respectively. The formation of nanocomposites causes different energy vibrations and shifting wavenumber of molecular bonding [53]. In addition, this result in accordance with the SEM-EDX result, confirmed Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites were successfully fabricated.

UV–vis is a required characterization to identify the formation of Cdots. Cdots have extended π-electron that support the fluorescence emission capability for bacteria detection [61]. Fig. 10 shows the absorption spectra of the samples. Cdots have strong absorption at 282 nm (UV region), attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of C = C [53]. Besides, Fe3O4 has an absorption shoulder characteristic peak at 370 nm. Both samples of Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposite have the same profile spectrum with the Fe3O4 but relatively reduce the absorbance intensity corresponding to Cdots concentration. Although the peak of Cdots on the nanocomposite is not too obvious, the reducing peak of Fe3O4 indicates the formation of Cdots on the surface of the magnetite. The band gap energy (Eg ) of the samples was examined using the Tauc plot equation [62]. The Eg of Cdots, Fe3O4, Fe3O4 /Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4 /Cdots 70 % are listed in Table 2 . Cdots have a higher Eg than other samples due to the small particle size. Adding Cdots to the Fe3O4 increases the Eg that is appropriate with TEM analysis. The small particle size has high band gap energy following the quantum confinement effect [51].

In this study, rapid bacteria detection was conducted using Photoluminescence (PL). Fig. 12 shows the PL intensity of Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % were obtained around 503 nm with the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. The higher Cdots concentration on the nanocomposites affects the higher intensity of PL. Cdots play an essential role in photoluminescence. It strengthened the above characterization that Cdots successfully attach on the surface of Fe3O4. The absorption phenomenon is related to π-transitions in the core of the Cdots, while the fluorescence in the visible light is due to photogenerated electrons and holes trapped at various surface sites and their associated radiative recombination [4]. The excellent photoluminescence properties of Cdots on the nanocomposites can be used for bacteria detection [5]. In this study, E.coli was used as a model with variation concentration to investigate bacteria detection capability, while the nanocomposite was in a constant concentration.

The addition of bacteria and the increasing bacteria concentration produce quenching PL intensity for both nanocomposites. The correlation between the PL intensity and the bacteria amount is appropriate for detection. The obvious quenching PL intensity can be seen in Fig. 12(b), the highest Cdots concentration produces high sensitivity detection. The quenching PL intensity is due to the interaction of nanocomposite with bacteria. The interaction between nanocomposites and bacteria occurs due to several reasons. First, the Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites have abundant hydrocarbon chain groups on the surface, which electrostatically interact with negatively charged E. coli [14]. In other work, the strong interaction occur between Cdots with proteins at the tips of fimbriae of E. coli [64]. According to Zheng et al., the functional group on Cdots also has a high binding affinity to E.coli [65]. Second, this phenomenon led to forming bacteria-nanocomposites conjugates, which reduced the photoluminescence intensity due to the transfer-like mechanism [66]. This means the E.coli-Cdots conjugates will dim the luminescence of Cdots. Hence, increasing E.coli concentration produces decreasing PL intensity. Although we cannot disregard the minor E. coli absorption, the PL responses will vary among other types of bacteria, indicating that the Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites are potential as an ON-OFF sensor for bacteria detection in the future. However, Zheng and co-workers used Cdots for bacteria detection and report that the different bacteria (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, D. desulfuricans, S. sciuri, L. monocytogenes and S. aureus) produce PL quenching with different responses [65]. The different responses are attributed to different binding abilities, sizes, morphologies, and surface chemical components of bacteria. Therefore, Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites is suitable candidate for rapid and broader bacteria detection applications, including both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria.

The antibacterial properties of Fe3O4/Cdots against Gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria were confirmed using Kirby–Bauer disk diffusion method. This experiment was conducted in 24 and 48 h incubation. The antibiotic amoxicillin was used as a standard or control positive due to the effective conventional antibacterial [67], and the distilled water was used as a control negative. The formation of a transparent zone shows good antibacterial activity, called the inhibition zone. Fig. 13 shows a large inhibition zone diameter surrounding the antibiotic, Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % and Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % samples. The antibiotic produce maximum inhibitory zone of about 2.45 cm after 48 h incubation. Fe3O4/Cdots 50 % achieved 1.82 cm after 24 h incubation and relatively constant after 48 h incubation. In addition, Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % earned more than 3 cm for 24 h incubation and increased to 4 cm after 48 h incubation, as shown in Table 4 . The inhibition zone produced by Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % are greater than the antibiotic. Compare to the Fe3O4/Cdots 50 %, the Fe3O4/Cdots 70 % also has superior antibacterial properties due to the higher Cdots concentration. It might be supported by the chemical stability and the smaller size of nanocomposites, leading to easier interaction with bacteria [27]. In the study by Ghaseminezhad et al, bare Fe3O4 has reduced bacterial growth, although it exhibited low activity [68]. Therefore, Cdots can enhance the antibacterial properties of nanocomposites.

The antibacterial mechanism was proposed by illustration in Fig. 14 . Generally, bacteria have a negative surface charge. E coli is a gram-negative bacteria with a thin peptidoglycan layer on the cell membrane. In the first step, Fe3O4/Cdots have electrostatic interaction with E.coli bacteria. In this process, Cdots plays an important role. Electrostatic interaction occurs because the Cdots on the surface of Fe3O4 have a positive surface charge, which can approach negatively charged bacteria surfaces [69]. Cdots on the surface of Fe3O4 also act as receptors to construct binding with E coli due to the rich functional group [65,70]. During the long time incubation, Cdots can initiate physical and mechanical damage to cell membranes [69]. Fe3O4 on the nanocomposite also supports the penetration to the cell wall [29,30]. The outer membrane or cell wall is responsible for the shape of the cells, preventing mechanical stress and osmotic adjustment [66]. If the membrane or cell wall is damaged, it will disrupt cell activity and membrane electron transport. Besides, it also leaks its cytoplasmic and other organelle cells, which will transfer out of the cell due to the difference in concentration between the inside and outside the cell. This mechanism propose the bacteriostatic and bactericidal effects, resulting large inhibition zone [71].

Moreover, Peng et al. describe another mechanism that could occur in the antibacterial activity. After penetration through the bacteria cell wall, Fe3O4 can release iron cation ions and cause reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation [72]. Beside, Qureshi et al. were reported that Cdots can induce bacterial cells to produce ROS [73]. ROS produced by the synergy of the two materials can cause oxidative stress, which can damage organelle cells and leading to cell death. According to previous study, the ROS produced might bind and affect the DNA in the bacteria cell, which interfere cell function [8,69,74]. Therefore, the diameter of the inhibition zone (DIZ) and the proposed mechanism show that adding Cdots to Fe3O4 plays an important role in producing nanocomposites with great potential as antibacterial agents. On the other hand, the combination of Cdots with Fe3O4 can easily be removed from the medium by applying a magnetic field. It supports the simple technique to remove agents from the bacterial medium in the future. Hence, using Fe3O4/Cdots prevents the uncontrolled waste disposal of antibacterial products in the environment [75].

Table 5 shows previous studies using the diffusion method of nanomaterial or nanocomposites as antibacterial agents. The bare Fe3O4 and Cdots have antibacterial activity against gram-negative bacteria, as well as gram-positive bacteria. The combination of Fe3O4 with Au and GO can increase the diameter of the inhibition zone (DIZ) by a small value. Besides, the combination of Cdots with Ag also produces DIZ less than 20 mm. Compared to other nanocomposites, the combination of Fe3O4 and Cdots produced synergetic activity to inhibit bacteria growth with several mechanisms. This is proven by the Fe3O4/Cdots, which achieved a DIZ of about 42 mm, the highest value among previous nanocomposites. Although this study only observed gram-negative bacteria (E. coli), the nanocomposites might be effective against other gram-positive bacteria due to the inherent properties of the two materials, each of which can react to both types of bacteria. Nanocomposites Fe3O4/Cdots were relatively stable, nano size and high specific surface area that encourages excellent antibacterial activity. The green synthesis method uses natural materials such as MO and watermelon peel, which promotes biocompatibility and environmentally friendly features. Furthermore, the stable magnetic properties of Fe3O4/Cdots had the potential to be recycled and reused for other detection and antibacterial processes.

Fe3O4/Cdots nanocomposites have been successfully fabricated via the green synthesis method utilizing MO leaves extract and watermelon peel waste. The nanocomposites have small sizes, large surface area, an abundance of hydroxyl bonds involving solubility, and superparamagnetic behavior that enhances the simple controllable material. In this study, green synthesized Fe3O4/Cdots were used for the first time as bacteria detection and antibacterial agents. Bacteria detection is performed by the quenching of photoluminescence of the nanocomposite according to increasing bacteria concentration. Nanocomposites demonstrated high inhibition zones against E. coli bacteria. Therefore, this nanocomposite can be used as a magnetically controllable bacteria detector and antibacterial agent in future biomedical applications.
